agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Delivery ETA presentation change from precise timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This affects how delivery promises are communicated to end customers and potentially how SLAs are measured internally.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "ETA", "definition": "Estimated Time of Arrival — the projected delivery completion time communicated to the customer" },
      { "term": "Delivery Window", "definition": "The time range within which a delivery is promised to arrive" },
      { "term": "SLA", "definition": "Service Level Agreement — contractual commitment on delivery timeliness, often measured against the ETA" },
      { "term": "On-Time Delivery Rate", "definition": "Percentage of deliveries completed within the promised ETA; a core operational KPI" },
      { "term": "Last Mile", "definition": "Final leg of delivery from distribution hub to recipient — the most variable and expensive segment" },
      { "term": "Delivery Promise", "definition": "The commitment shown to the customer at time of order or in tracking — what the business is accountable for" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A delivery promise must always be communicated to the customer before or at order confirmation",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without a delivery promise, the customer has no basis for the transaction and the business has no accountability target",
      "testable_assertion": "Every confirmed order has a non-null ETA visible to the customer"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "The ETA format must be unambiguous — the customer must be able to determine when to expect delivery without interpretation",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Ambiguous ETAs erode trust, increase support contacts, and create legal exposure. A promise that can't be understood isn't a promise.",
      "testable_assertion": "'Delivery by Friday' must resolve to a specific calendar date (not 'this Friday' vs 'next Friday') and must include end-of-day semantics (e.g., by 23:59 local time)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Changing ETA granularity must not silently alter SLA measurement — if SLAs are measured against hourly precision internally, the display change must not mask SLA failures",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Decoupling the customer-facing promise from internal measurement creates a integrity gap where the business appears to hit targets while actually degrading service",
      "testable_assertion": "Internal SLA reporting retains timestamp-level precision regardless of customer-facing display format"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Premium/time-sensitive delivery tiers (same-day, express, scheduled) must retain time-level precision in their ETA",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a product policy — a VP could decide premium tiers also use date windows — but removing time precision from premium tiers fundamentally undermines the value proposition customers pay extra for",
      "testable_assertion": "Orders with delivery tier = 'same-day', 'express', or 'scheduled' continue to display hour-level or time-window ETAs"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Date-only ETAs must specify the date unambiguously using a full calendar date, not relative terms like 'tomorrow' or 'Friday' alone",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Relative dates create ambiguity across time zones and near day boundaries; this is a UX policy that could be relaxed with sufficient context",
      "testable_assertion": "ETA display includes at minimum the day-of-week AND calendar date (e.g., 'Friday, March 27')"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A standard-tier delivery order is confirmed",
      "when": "The customer views the order confirmation or tracking page",
      "then": "The ETA is displayed as a full calendar date (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday, March 27') with no hour/minute component"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A premium-tier delivery (same-day, express, scheduled) order is confirmed",
      "when": "The customer views the order confirmation or tracking page",
      "then": "The ETA retains time-level precision (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday, March 27' or a time window like '2:00–4:00 PM')"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "An order is in transit with a date-only ETA",
      "when": "The delivery is completed at any time on the promised date (00:00–23:59 local recipient time)",
      "then": "The delivery is recorded as on-time"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "The ETA format changes from timestamp to date-only",
      "when": "Internal SLA dashboards and reporting are queried",
      "then": "SLA metrics continue to track against the original timestamp-level precision, not the relaxed date-only window"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "An existing order was confirmed with a timestamp-level ETA before the change",
      "when": "The format change is deployed",
      "then": "The order retains its original timestamp-level ETA — no retroactive downgrade of the delivery promise"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A date-only ETA is displayed",
      "when": "The customer is in any time zone",
      "then": "The date shown is relative to the recipient's local time zone, not UTC or sender's time zone"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Order placed at 11:55 PM — 'Delivery by Friday' could mean today or tomorrow depending on timezone handling", "expected_behavior": "Date resolves to the recipient's local calendar date; display must never show a date that has already passed for the recipient" },
    { "scenario": "Cross-border delivery spanning multiple time zones", "expected_behavior": "ETA date is always in the recipient's local time zone" },
    { "scenario": "Delivery rescheduled after a failed attempt — new ETA must still be date-only for standard tier", "expected_behavior": "Rescheduled ETA follows the same format rules; the new date is shown clearly as an update" },
    { "scenario": "Customer has both a premium and standard order in progress", "expected_behavior": "Each order displays the ETA format appropriate to its tier — no confusion from mixed formats in the same UI" },
    { "scenario": "SLA report compares on-time rate before and after the format change", "expected_behavior": "Historical data retains original timestamp-based SLA measurement; the apparent on-time rate should not jump due to looser customer-facing windows" },
    { "scenario": "API consumers (partners, integrations) that parse the ETA field", "expected_behavior": "API response must include both the display string and a machine-readable ISO-8601 field; breaking the API contract is unacceptable" },
    { "scenario": "'Delivery by Friday' shown on Thursday at 11 PM when the package won't arrive until Saturday", "expected_behavior": "ETA must be recalculated and updated in real-time; showing a stale date-only ETA that is about to be missed is worse than showing a delayed timestamp" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Moderate positive short-term: fewer SLA breach compensations as date-only windows are easier to hit than precise timestamps, reducing refund/credit payouts. However, this is an artificial improvement — it masks operational issues rather than fixing them. If premium tiers are accidentally included, revenue from premium delivery surcharges is at risk as customers lose the value they paid for. Long-term risk of reduced conversion if customers perceive less precise ETAs as less reliable.",
    "operational": "Significant positive: reduces pressure on dispatch and last-mile teams who currently must hit hourly targets. Reduces customer support contacts about 'my delivery was supposed to arrive at 2:30 and it's now 3:00.' However, if internal SLA tracking is also relaxed (which this change must NOT do), operational visibility degrades and problems go undetected longer. Route optimization and capacity planning systems that depend on hourly ETA data must continue to function at timestamp precision internally.",
    "user": "Mixed impact. Negative for customers who plan their day around delivery times — losing hour-level precision means they can't schedule around the delivery. Especially painful for B2B recipients, office deliveries with closing hours, and customers in buildings requiring someone present. Positive for customers who were frustrated by narrowly missed timestamp ETAs that triggered 'late' notifications. Net effect depends heavily on the customer segment mix.",
    "risk": "Medium-high. (1) Contractual risk: B2B/enterprise contracts may specify timestamp-level SLAs — changing the display without contract review could create disputes. (2) Competitive risk: competitors offering precise time windows will appear more reliable. (3) Measurement risk: if internal teams also shift to date-only measurement, real service degradation goes undetected. (4) Migration risk: existing orders with timestamp promises must not be retroactively downgraded. (5) API breaking change risk: partners consuming ETA fields may break if format changes without versioning.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "End Customers (B2C)", "impact": "Lose ability to plan around precise delivery time; some will perceive this as a service downgrade. Support inquiries may initially increase as customers ask 'but WHEN on Friday?'" },
      { "group": "End Customers (B2B/Enterprise)", "impact": "High impact — office hours, loading dock scheduling, and staffing depend on time-level precision. Date-only may be unacceptable for contractual commitments." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Short-term increase in 'when exactly?' inquiries, but medium-term reduction in 'it's 5 minutes late' complaints. Net positive after adjustment period." },
      { "group": "Delivery Drivers/Couriers", "impact": "Reduced pressure to hit exact timestamps; more flexibility in route sequencing within a day. Positive for driver satisfaction and safety." },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Must maintain internal timestamp-level tracking while presenting date-only externally. Adds a translation layer. Risk of confusion if teams start treating date-only as the real target." },
      { "group": "Product/Commercial Team", "impact": "Premium tier differentiation becomes more important — must ensure premium tiers are explicitly excluded from this change or the premium value proposition collapses." },
      { "group": "API Partners/Integrators", "impact": "Breaking change if ETA field format changes. Must be communicated via API versioning or additive field changes." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue", "impact": "Short-term savings from fewer SLA breach credits. Risk of revenue loss if premium tier is affected or if conversion drops due to perceived service downgrade." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The proposal lacks critical details: (1) Which delivery tiers are affected — all tiers or only standard? (2) Whether this is a display-only change or also changes internal SLA measurement. (3) Whether B2B/enterprise contracts have been reviewed. (4) Whether the API contract changes. (5) What motivated this change — cost reduction, accuracy issues, or competitive repositioning? Without knowing the motivation and scope, the analysis must make assumptions that may be wrong.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Date-only ETAs reduce on-time delivery pressure and SLA breach payouts",
      "Premium/express tiers must retain time precision or their value proposition collapses",
      "Internal SLA measurement must not be relaxed alongside the display change",
      "Existing orders must not have their promises retroactively downgraded",
      "API consumers will be affected and need migration support"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether B2B contracts mandate timestamp-level ETAs (legal/contract review needed)",
      "Net user satisfaction impact — depends on customer segment mix not provided",
      "Whether this is motivated by inaccurate timestamp predictions or cost reduction",
      "Scope of the change — which tiers, which channels (app, email, API, SMS)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Premium delivery tiers are excluded from this change",
      "Internal SLA tracking remains at timestamp precision",
      "This is a display-layer change, not a change to the underlying ETA calculation engine",
      "The change applies to customer-facing channels (app, web, email) but API needs separate handling"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The proposal does not specify which delivery tiers are affected. If premium/express tiers are included, this is a fundamentally different and much riskier change than if only standard delivery is affected.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["End Customers (B2B)", "End Customers (B2C)", "Product/Commercial Team", "Finance/Revenue"],
      "options": ["A: Apply date-only to standard tier only, retain timestamps for premium/express/scheduled", "B: Apply date-only to all tiers", "C: Apply date-only to standard, widen to time-windows (e.g., 2-hour blocks) for premium"],
      "recommendation": "Option A. Premium tiers derive their value from time precision. Removing it destroys the pricing justification."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "It is unclear whether this change also affects internal SLA measurement or is display-only. If SLA measurement also shifts to date-level, the business loses operational visibility into service quality trends.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations/Dispatch", "Finance/Revenue", "End Customers (B2B)"],
      "options": ["A: Display-only change; internal SLAs remain timestamp-based", "B: Both display and SLA measurement shift to date-only"],
      "recommendation": "Option A. Internal measurement must remain at timestamp precision to detect degradation. Display and measurement must be decoupled."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No information provided about B2B/enterprise contracts. Many enterprise logistics contracts specify timestamp-level delivery commitments. Changing the display without contract review could create legal disputes or breach of contract.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["End Customers (B2B/Enterprise)", "Legal", "Finance/Revenue"],
      "options": ["Proceed after legal/contract review of all enterprise SLAs", "Exclude B2B/enterprise accounts from the change entirely"],
      "recommendation": "Do not proceed for any B2B/enterprise accounts until contracts are reviewed. Apply change to B2C standard tier only as a first phase."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The business motivation for this change is not stated. If the driver is inaccurate timestamp predictions, the correct fix may be improving prediction accuracy rather than hiding the imprecision. If the driver is reducing SLA breach costs, this is a band-aid that masks operational problems.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations/Dispatch", "Product/Commercial Team", "End Customers (B2C)"],
      "options": ["Improve ETA prediction accuracy instead of reducing precision", "Accept reduced precision as a deliberate product decision", "Hybrid: improve prediction AND widen windows as a safety margin"],
      "recommendation": "Clarify the root cause before proceeding. If predictions are inaccurate, invest in prediction quality. If the business consciously wants simpler communication, proceed with the display change for standard tier only."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
